Read Wiki Edit Wiki

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This module allows accessing the mapping of financial instruments relevant to sustainable space-cooling solutions. The users can filter the mapping per country, including or not the EU-level schemes, per instrument source (public, private or all), or access the whole CoolLIFE EU27 Mapping of Financing Instruments mapping on the CoolLIFE Website.

Each scheme is classified and provided with details per country, level (European, National, Regional, Local), Name in English and local language, issuer (public or private), covered sectors (Y= directly ; (Y)= indirectly; blank= not covered), type of instrument, main and additional links, a short description and the last time the page was visited.

For private schemes, if a suitable instrument is not currently available, it is interesting to note that anyway the credit and investment institutes listed in the mapping are engaged in green finance and could offer other options or be open to bilateral negotiations.

docs.title: Input
docs.text: The user selects on the map a region at the NUTS1 until LAU2 levels. The CM gets activated in the CM menu and the user can select it. The user then selects in the CM box if the EU-level schemes should be included, and if the schemes should be filtered per public, private or all.

docs.title: Output indicators and layers
docs.text:
Running the CM provides in the result box the total number of schemes, the link to the overall mapping and all the scheme titles for the filtered region/selection. An additional layer can be found in the layer tab, which can be downloaded as a zip file, including a selection-specific Excel and CSV file.

Note: The mapping was last updated in August 2024. Some schemes might have been discontinued and some new schemes might have been launched which are not captured in this mapping. For more detail, please consult the CoolLIFE D4.2 Review of Financing Schemes and the full dataset CoolLIFE EU27 Mapping of Financing Instruments which list details on the mapping process and references where new schemes might easily be found.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
In the following chapter, we will describe how to access a calculation module

Steps to access a calculation module

cm_access.gif

Steps:

  1. Define a territorial scale
  2. Select a region
  3. Enable the Layer Section
  4. Go to the Calculation Modules Tab
  5. Access your desired module
  6. Define the parameters, give a name to the run and run the module
  7. results are shown in the results bar and in the map cm_access.png

docs.title: Structure of a calculation module
docs.text:
The picture below shows the structure of a calculation module

cm_structure_png

docs.title: List of Calculation Modules
docs.text: Below you see the currently available modules. In the lower part, the stand-alone calculation modules are listed. Each one has a link to his dedicated wiki page or separate wiki and to his repository, as they are not an integral part of the CoolLIFE Toolbox.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This CM allows visualization of space cooling demand for the whole territory of the EU27 on a resolution of 100 x 100 m for the base year as well as for scenarios up to 2050. It is based on the data based on building stock distribution and climate data. With the CM, the user will be able to visualize and scale up demand to identify cold spots in cities, which could be priorities of cooling planning.

This module scales the default layer with a given factor. Often cold demands are reported in an aggregated manner in energy balances. Using this calculation module, it is possible to use the spatial distribution of cold demand density maps to disaggregate such values. This is done by scaling the default cool density maps up or to reach the desired value. The calculation module provides a new raster by scaling a raster density map by a user-chosen factor. It generates a new raster by multiplying each cell of the input raster by the given factor.

Go To Top

docs.title: Inputs and Outputs
docs.text: The input parameters and layers, as well as output layers and parameters, are as follows.

docs.title: Input layers and parameters are:
docs.text:
• Multiplication factor [-]: a real value between 0 and 1000
  • if the multiplication factor is > 1, the output raster is greater than the input.
  • if the multiplication factor is < 1, the output raster is smaller than the input.
  • The layer to be scaled:
  • Cool density map in raster format (*.tif): It is possible to scale personal heat and cool density maps up or down as well.

docs.title: Output layers and parameters are:
docs.text: • An output raster (*.tif), corresponding to the input scaled by the multiplication factor.

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
Each cell's value of the input raster is multiplied by the multiplication factor.

Go To Top

docs.title: Sample Run
docs.text:
Here, the calculation module is run for the case study of Vienna, Austria. First, use the "Go To Place" bar to navigate to Vienna and select the city. Click on the "Layers" button to open the "Layers" window and then click on the "CALCULATION MODULE" tab. In the list of calculation modules, select "CM - Space Cooling Demand".

Once you are in the CM, select the value by the amount you want to scale up or down in the Multiplication Factor input. By default, the scaling factor is 1. Press "RUN CM". Once the calculation is completed, the scaled-up demand density map will be visible on the map, and the newly calculated demand will be available in the result section. If you want to download this layer, go to the "Layers" tab, scroll to the bottom under the "Calculation module" section, and you will find the layer generated from the CM run as "Layers of Cold_density"

Go To Top

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This calculation module provides information on the energy consumption of the technologies that are installed to supply the cooling needs in EU-27 area. The information is available for either the residential or non-residential sector (or both simultaneously). The residential sector comprises single family houses, multifamily houses and appartment blocks. The non-residential sector comprises buildings of offices, trades, education, health, hotels and restaurants sectors as well as other non-residential buildings.

The calculation module also offers the possibility to observe the impacts of measures uptake to mitigate the cooling needs in both sectors. Finally, the user can also access the cooling capacity that is needed to supply the considered cooling needs.

Go To Top

docs.title: Inputs layers
docs.text:
The calculation module relies on the 3 following layers:

  • Cooling degree days, showing the Cooling Degree Days for the reference period 2002-2012 in EU28 on hectare (ha) level.

Information on the layer can be found here - https://gitlab.com/hotmaps/climate/CDD_ha_curr/blob/master/README.md

  • Gross floor area residential, showing the residential gross floor area in EU28 on hectare (ha) level.

Information on the layer can be found here: https://gitlab.com/hotmaps/gfa_res_curr_density/blob/master/README.md

  • Gross floor area non-residential, showing the residential gross floor area in EU28 on hectare (ha) level.

Information on the layer can be found here: https://gitlab.com/hotmaps/gfa_nonres_curr_density/blob/master/README.md

docs.title: User inputs
docs.text:
The user shall also give the following inputs (defauts value are also suggested).

Building sector selection, indicates the building sector selected in the current calculation. There are 3 options: residential, non-residential and both sectors

Default value: residential

Space cooling diffusion rate [%], indicates the share (in %) of the cooled area among the total floor area of the selected sector

Default value: 7,5 % in the residential sector and 23,5 % in the non-residential sector (average values of cooled area in EU27)

Average SEER of SC technologies [-], indicates the average (in installed capacity) performance of the stock of space cooling technologies.

Default value: 4,7 in the residential sector and 4,4 in the non-residential sector (average values of installed cooling capacities in EU27)

Measures uptake scenario, represents the level of adoption by the buildings' occupants of measures that reduce the space cooling needs.

  • No measure: the buildings' occupants take no action to reduce the cooling needs

  • Low adoption: the buildings' occupants moderatly use a mix of measures to reduce the cooling needs. 40% of occupants use systematically windows opening strategies for reducing the cooling needs and 20% among us also use both fans and shading.

  • High adoption: the buildings' occupants widely use a mix of measures to reduce the cooling needs. 80% of occupants use systematically windows opening strategies for reducing the cooling needs and 40% among us also use both fans and shading.

Default value: no measure

Go To Top

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
The calculation module shows the following indicators:

Residential or non-residential cooled area [m2]: gives the residential and/or non-residential sectors area which is equipped with space cooling technologies.

Cooling demand [MWh/year]: gives the cooling demand of the selected cooled area (ie. of the residential and/or non-residential cooled area). The specific cooling demands for both sectors depend on the average value of CDD in the selected area and are derived from [Dittmann, 2017]

Cooling demand with measures [MWh/year]: gives the cooling demand reduced by the impact of measures implemented by the occupants. The above cooling demand is reduced by two factors (for each measure) : the adoption rate and the efficiency of the measures which are extracted from [Duplessis, 2023]

Space cooling final energy consumption [MWh/year]: gives the electricity consumption of the space cooling technologies. In the current release of CoolLIFE tool, this output is based on an average SEER of SC technologies stock at EU27 scale [Dittmann, 2017]

Cooling capacity [MW]: gives the cooling capacity installed in the selected cooled area. The specific cooling capacities for both sectors depend on the average value of CDD in the selected area and are derived from [Dittmann, 2017]

Cooling power capacity [MW]: based on the cooling capacity, the power capacity of the installed SC technologies is based on an average SEER of SC technologies stock at EU27 scale [Dittmann, 2017]

Go To Top

docs.title: References
docs.text:
[Dittmann, 2017] Space Cooling Technology in Europe: Technology Data and Demand Modelling - HRE Deliverable 3.2, Florian Dittmann, Philippe Rivière, Pascal Stabat, Heat Roadmap Europe project, 2017

[Duplessis, 2023] Taxonomy of space cooling technologies and measures - CoolLIFE Deliverable 2.1, Bruno Duplessis, Essam Elnagar, Théodore Fontenaille, Adrienn Gelesz, Riccardo Fraboni, Valentina Radice Fossati, Open Source Tools to Face the Increase in Buildings’ Space Cooling Demand - CoolLIFE project, 2023

Go To Top

docs.title: CM 3: Comfort, Lifestyle, And User Behaviour
Introduction
docs.text:
Go To Top

This calculation module provides information for the user on typical summer thermal comfort requirements and expectations, typical lifestyle and user behavioural patterns that influence space cooling (SC) demand within the buildings through the different member states of the EU27 area. Additionally, active behavioural SC interventions and adaptive routine practices that have proven to be successful in reducing SC demand in Europe and globally are displayed.

The calculation module provides the following types of information: - traditional behavioural patterns and lifestyle choices that are adopted in the country specific climatic context, confirmed by empirical studies, - summer thermal comfort expectations of the building occupants, demonstrated through legislative and standard based setpoints or overheating limits, - summer thermal comfort expectations reported in empirical studies, - and successful intervention strategies that have proven to lead to increased acceptance of the thermal environment, or reduction of space cooling demand.

The CM is available on multiple scales. The user can select one or more territorial areas from the map using the selection tool. The calculation module is available on the NUTS0 - NUTS3, LAU and Hectare levels. The result provided correspond to the country level.

docs.title: Inputs and outputs
docs.text: Go To Top

docs.title: Traditional behavioural patterns and lifestyle choices
docs.text:
As part of human adaptation to hot temperatures (mainly in regions in a warmer climatic zone), traditional practices passed down through generations have been mainly focused on features of the building design that helped people to live comfortably in the hot periods of the year. The passive and lifestyle measures for coping with summer weather and high temperatures have been compiled during the CoolLIFE project. The geographical distribution of traditional behavioural practices and passive solutions is incorporated into this module from the literature. Please see D3.1 section D4.4 in more detail.

The user can select whether these should be displayed or not.

docs.title: Thermal expectations
docs.text:
Thermal expectations on the national level were collected and summarized during the CoolLIFE project, published in deliverables D3.1 and D3.2 and D4.1. Information for legislative and standard values are displayed as indicators. Custom indicators are displayed for each country, aligned with the country specific legislative environment, for example, legislative setpoints values for areas with space cooling, or overheating limits for specific building types. Indicator values are stored and retrieved from the database on NUTS0 level.

Additionally, preferred indoor temperatures in summer for residential buildings, where available, are displayed in the Charts tab. The current version of the tool includes these values for Germany, France, Spain and Hungary, aligned with the availability of results from representative surveys.

docs.title: Behavioural interventions
docs.text:
Behavioural interventions collected and presented in the D3.2 deliverable are presented in the indicator list. The user has the possibility to filter the interventions based on type of behaviour, the type of building and the type of intervention. Users can select a specific option from the dropdown menu, or can select to display all of the interventions. Additionally, the users can determine whether they would like to restrain the search for interventions that have been proven to be successful in published case studies within the country of interest, or they would like to see all case studies regardless of the geographical context.

The type of behaviour explains what behaviour was targetted of the intervention. This can be selected from the following list: - thermostat setpoints, - shading and lighting, - adaptation, - electricity-powered space cooling appliances, - electricity use - space use.

The building type categories have been aligned with the available case studies, and can be selected from: - residential - office - public - educational - unspecified non-residential - unspecified.

The intervention type categories have been aligned with the literature review done within the project, and can be selected from: - feedback and information, - monetary incentives, - nudges, - policy.

Based on the selected combination, information regarding the successful interventions will be displayed on the indicator tab. The displayed data contains a more detailed description of the intervention, details on the results of the behavioural change, the geographical context of the case studies and a link to the scientific publication of the case study results.

docs.title: Sources
docs.text:
Go To Top

CoolLIFE project deliverables:

Laura Hurtado-Verazaín, Adrienn Gelesz, Adrienne Csizmady Henriett Szabó, Zoltán Ferencz, Anikó Vincze, Lea Kőszeghy, Nicolas Caballero, András Reith: D3.1 Knowledgebase for occupant-centric space cooling, section 4.5. Differences in space cooling-related comfort requirements

Adrienn Gelesz, András Reith, Henriett Szabó, Laura Hurtado-Verazaín, Lukas Kranzl, Bruno Duplessis, Nicolas Caballero, Simon Pezzutto: D3.2. Analysis of behavioural interventions in Europe

D4.1 - under finalization

docs.title: In a glance
docs.text: Calculation module on economic and financial assessment: This module will provide the option to do an economic and financial assessment of a certain investment in SC facilities, considering different financing opportunities. Either based on default data on typical investment costs in €/kW of various technologies or considering more specific information of concrete project data the user may derive key financial information - based on cost-benefit analysis (CBA) indications on the project considering different financing support options.

docs.title: National Level:
docs.text:
The national-level assessment is designed for energy planners to formulate and analyze various policy scenarios, focusing on the evolution of cooling demand and associated costs.

This tool allows users to compare demand trajectories under current policy frameworks with scenarios that envision both high and low uptake of active cooling technologies. It also examines how advancements in technology efficiency impact overall demand over time. For financial assessments, the tool evaluates the costs associated with energy savings and investments in active cooling technologies.

Key parameters used in our analysis include the Levelized Cost of Cooling (LCOC) for cooling supply and the Levelized Cost of Energy Savings (LCES). By comparing these costs at a national scale, the tool helps determine the economic viability of investing in both passive and active cooling measures, guiding strategic decision-making in energy policy development.

docs.title: Building Level:
docs.text: Building level assessment will soon be added to the calculation module.

docs.title: Method
docs.text: The approach allows the quantification of the effects of passive measures and technological advancements on space cooling demands, integrating environmental, economic, and societal impacts. The methodology is structured to utilize open-source data sets for evaluating EU-wide cooling demand trends, starting with a bottom-up estimation based on national building stock distribution. The process involves calibrating building physics parameters to reflect passive cooling measures, leading to estimates of Theoretical Useful Energy Demand (TUED) and Practical Useful Energy Demand (PUED).

docs.title: Estimation of Theoretical Useful Energy Demand (TUED):
docs.text: This step models the potential cooling demand if all built floor area had space cooling technologies, serving as a baseline to assess energy savings from passive measures. This is taken as input from results generated by the Invert EE-lab model

docs.title: Diffusion Rates of Technology:
docs.text: The adoption rates of active cooling technologies are analyzed to translate theoretical potential into practical application, reflecting realistic market penetration.

docs.title: Final Energy Demand Assessment:
docs.text: After considering the adoption of passive and active technologies, the actual energy consumption (Final Energy Demand) is estimated, accounting for different rates of technological advancement and the mix of technologies employed.

docs.title: Impact of Passive Measures:
docs.text:
The methodology rigorously accounts for the potential energy savings achievable through various scenarios of passive measure uptake, allowing for detailed projections of energy demand reductions across different EU regions and timeframes.

This approach not only provides insights into the direct impacts of cooling technologies but also highlights their broader implications for energy efficiency and social well-being within the EU-27 context.

docs.title: Economic Parameters
docs.text:
• Active CAPEX: The investment in new technology for each year is annualized over its lifetime.

  • Active OPEX

  • Passive CAPEX

docs.title: Input layers and parameters
docs.text:
• Layers
  • The calculation module works on a national level NUTS 0. The user needs to select the region where the analysis is to be performed. In case of an error regarding the size of the region, the user can select a sub-region (e.g. NUTS1) within the same country, which will still give results on the national level. The user needs to avoid selecting multiple regions at the same time.
  • Parameter
  • Assessment type: The user can perform the assessment either on a national level or a building level. The national level covers the overall cooling sector of the selected country, whereas the building level provides financial indicators to a homeowner regarding investment in space cooling measures and technologies.
  • Passive measure efficiency: Based on literature and existing market shares of different passive cooling measures, options on pre-defined passive measures packages at different levels of efficiency can be selected. The future possibility of user-defined packages is foreseen.
Category Baseline Moderate High Shading No additional shading Manual shading Automated shading Shading Technology Internal Venetian Blind Highly reflective inner screen External Venetian Blind Window Glazing Single glazing window Double glazing low emissivity window Solar controlled window glazing Night Ventilation No night ventilation Low level of night ventilation High level of night ventilation Indoor Temperature Average indoor temperature Indoor temperature increase by 2°C Indoor temperature increased by 4°C
  • Diffusion rates of Active Measures: The parameter indicates the rate of diffusion of cooling technologies. Scenarios can be developed based on different rates of diffusion of the technology.
  • Active measure efficiency: This parameter indicates the SEER values of the individual supply technology, allowing the user to define the development of cooling technologies.

docs.title: Output Parameters:
docs.text:
• Parameters
  • Total Final Energy demand from the scenario
  • Useful Energy saving potential from the selected scenario in the year 2050
  • Percentage Final Energy demand savings
  • Description of the Scenario
  • Charts
  • Total Useful Energy demand of the scenario vs. Baseline for years 2030,2040 and 2050
  • Total Final Energy demand of the scenario vs. Baseline for years 2030,2040 and 2050
  • Break down of total investment in active and passive measures (scenario vs. Baseline)
  • Comparison of levelized cost of cooling (Passive - Euros per MWh of saved useful energy demand; Active - Euros per MWh of supplied useful energy demand)

docs.title: Sample Run
docs.text: Here, the sample run of the calculation module for Austria is performed on a national level. 1. Area type selection: Select the NUTS0 on the top right area selection option. 2. Region Selection option: Select the NUTS0 region Austria 3. Go to the calculation module tab and select the CM-Economic Feasibility 4. Now, we provide the input parameters to the CM. Here, we have simplified the user input requirements with a set of pre-defined combinations of technology and measures based on literature. - Select the Assessment Tyoe as National as we are performing a National level assessment. The results from the CM could be an input to the National Comprehensive Assessment Report. - Select the passive measure efficiency. Choose 'High Efficiency'. This means that we are assessing a scenario where we see a widespread application of passive measures with high efficiency (energy savings potential) in Austria up to 2050. The underlying assumptions and passive measure combination details are in the method section above. - Select Difussion Rates of Active Measures. Choose 'Moderate Efficiency'. This means we are assessing a scenario where there is a moderate increase in the diffusion of technology until 2050. The underlying assumptions and rates of active measure uptakes are available in the method section above. - Select Active Measure Efficiency. Choose 'High Efficiency'. This means we are assessing a scenario where there is considerable improvement in the efficiency of the active cooling technology. The underlying assumptions and rates of active measure efficiency improvements are available in the method section above. - Define the discount rate. For this example, we assume 5%. - Lifetime of the technology and measures can be adjusted, based on literature and average value define this as 10 years for this example 5. Run the CM 6. Example Results: - Baseline SEER: Based on literature the current SEER value for Austria. Future scenarios are based on this - Base year Useful energy demand for Base Scenario: This is the comparison scenario where no passive and active measure uptake and no efficiency measures are assumed for the entire study horizon. - 2050 Useful energy demand for Base Scenario: This is the projected 2050 demand for the base scenario. - 2050 Useful energy demand for Selected Scenario: This is the projected 2050 demand for the scenario we have defined based on the input parameters. - FED savings estimated in the scenario 2050: Compared to the baseline, here are the savings in the final energy demand with the defined uptake and development of active and passive cooling measures. - Scenario Description: Definition of the defined scenario. - Chart 1: Comparison of Baseline and selected scenario over the time horizon - Chart 2: Break down of the total investment required for the selected scenario compared against the baseline - Chart 3: Comparison of levelized cost of cooling on useful energy demand of the two scenarios. In this example, it is obvious that the passive measures are more appealing, and hence, it could be argued to orient the policy framework toward promoting its uptake.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Go To Top

The CoolLife Demand-side Management/Demand Response calculation module) provides users with quick and accurate impression of cooling demand response potential. The CM is designed to assess the DR potential of cooling demand for a user-defined region by utilizing pre-cooling strategies and the thermal properties of the region’s building stock to provide an analysis of PV self-consumption, where DR is used to shift cooling demand to match PV supply.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This module provides access to policy-relevant data collected within the scope of the CoolLIFE project. The users can search for information about the current EU policy framework and national legislation, planning and strategies relevant to sustainable space cooling solutions.

The module summarizes information available in EU legislation, as well as in Member State's reporting to the European Commission about strategies, policies and regulations relevant to sustainable space cooling, complemented by targeted searches on national building regulations and financing schemes (public and private).

By now, information is included for the following EU Member States: Austria, Croatia, France, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Italy and Spain. More countries will be added in the coming weeks.

docs.title: Input layers
docs.text:
Step 1: The user selects on the map the region whose data is required. This can be done for all NUTS regions.

For this module, you can select NUTS1 (in "Selectable areas" on the top right), and then select any region in the country you are interested in.

Note 1: If you receive an AREA SELECTION WARNING, we recommend choosing a lower NUTS level within the same country. This warning usually occurs when the selected area is too large. Opting for smaller regions within the country should resolve the issue, as the data you access are on a national level.

Note 2: If you want to see the information about another country, first click again on the region previously selected (to de-select it), and then click on another region in the country you are interested in.

Step 2: The user goes in "Calculation Modules" in the menu on the bottom left. Then select "CM - Legal and Regulatory Layers" under "Policy".

Step 3: The user selects the type of information to be displayed in the tool.

This is done in "INPUTS", by selecting the "Indicator of Interest" See below Outputs indicators and layers about the different indicators available.

Note: "Import CM inputs" appears in the panel of this CM, as for any CM. However, for this CM, it is not needed and not possible for users to import their own data.

Step 4: The user runs the CM by clicking on "RUN CM" at the bottom of the CM panel.

Then the information about the selected indicator will appear in a new "RESULTS" window on the right. You can scroll down to see the whole information available about the selected indicator.

To see the information about another indicator, you can select this other indicator in "Indicator of Interest" and click again on "RUN CM".

If you are interested in getting all information at once with more details, you can download the country file (see Output indicators and layers below).

docs.title: Output indicators and layers
docs.text:
Go To Top

  • Running the CM generates a summary on background and policies in the selected country, structured according to "Indicators of Interest":

National background and strategy

Governance

Links with Adaptation

EU planning processes

National planning processes

How space cooling is addressed in the building regulations

Main provisions relevant to space cooling needs or summer comfort

Main provisions relevant to the use of space cooling

EU regulations and provisions

National implementation

Overview of public schemes

Overview of private schemes

  • If the user needs more detailed data or all data at once, the Calculation Module (CM) can also generate an additional layer in the layer tab to download the country file.

Step 1: the user goes in "Layers" in the menu on the bottom left.

Step 2: either use the search box and enter "policy", or scroll down the layers until "Calculation Module". Then you should see the blue-ticked box for the "POLICY - EXCEL - TABLE"

Step 3: click on the cloud icon to download the file (you can see "Download CM result" when passing your mouse over the cloud icon).

Step 4: when unzipping the file on your disk, you will obtain a series of 5 files. The country file is the first file (Excel format, with ".xlsx" extension). The structure of the country files is described below.

Note: The data collection process is ongoing, and data on more countries will become available soon (see list of countries available at the beginning of this page).

docs.title: Structure of the Datasets
docs.text: Go To Top

docs.title: How space cooling is addressed in the building regulations
Main provisions relevant to space cooling needs or summer comfort
Main provisions relevant to the use of space cooling
Structure of the country files (attachment to download)
docs.text:
Go To Top

This Excel file gathers information available in the Member State's reporting to the European Commission about strategies, policies and regulations relevant to sustainable space cooling, and a focus on the national building regulations and financing schemes (public and private).

It includes the following spreadsheets: - [Summary] provides an overview of the main information from all sources reviewed - [draftNECPupdate] provides the information identified in the draft update of National Energy Climate Plan (2023) - [CA2020] provides the information identified in the Comprehensive Assessment for heating and cooling (2020 version) - [BuildingRegulation] provides the information identified in the national building regulations - [Public Schemes] and [Private Schemes] provide two lists of financing schemes that promote energy efficiency in buildings and/or efficient space cooling systems - [Adaptation] provides the information identified in the National Adaptation Strategy and/or National Adaptation Plan

docs.title: Authors And Reviewers
docs.text:
Go To Top

The dataset was compiled by Jean-Sébastien Broc (IEECP), and computed by Aadit Malla (TU Wien).

Data about financing schemes (public and private) has been prepared by Giulia Conforto (E-THINK) and reviewed by CoolLIFE partners.

Data about adaptation has been prepared by Indriyani Liongo (IEECP).

The other data has been collected on a country-per-country basis: * Austria: Aadit Malla (TU Wien) * Croatia: Petra Pomper (IEECP) * France: Bruno Dupessis (ARMINES / MinesParistech) and Jean-Sébastien Broc (IEECP) * Germany: Giulia Conforto (E-THINK) * Greece: Vassilis Stavrakas and Dimitris Athanasiou (IEECP) * Hungary: Adrienn Gelesz (ABUD) * Italy: Dario Bottino (EURAC) * Spain: Jean-Sébastien Broc (IEECP)

Quality Assurance was done by Jean-Sébastien Broc (IEECP).

docs.title: First Steps
docs.text:
To be able to edit this wiki, make sure that:

  • ✅ You have a GitHub account.
  • ✅ You are logged in to GitHub.
  • ✅ You have been added as a contributor to this wiki.

docs.title: Edit Page
docs.text:
Go To Top

  • Click Edit Page at top right.
  • Edit content in GitHub wiki frontend.
  • Use Preview to see your changes.
  • Click Save Page at bottom right.
  • Changes take time to be visible in read frontend.

docs.title: Edit Page Title
docs.text:
Go To Top

  • Click Edit Page at top right.
  • Edit part after title: in GitHub wiki frontend.
  • Use Preview to see your changes.
  • Click Save Page at bottom right.
  • Changes take time to be visible in read frontend.

  • Click Save Page at bottom right.
  • Changes take time to be visible in read frontend.

docs.title: Delete Page
docs.text:
Go To Top

  • Click Edit Page at top right.
  • Click Delete page in GitHub wiki frontend.
  • Confirm Are you sure? dialogue.
  • Changes take time to be visible in read frontend.

docs.title: Retrieve Indicators Of A Selected Area
docs.text:
When the user loads a layer or runs a calculation module, the results appear on the right side of the screen in the results section. The results consist of two parts: Indicators and Charts. The indicators illustrate the quantitative values, whereas different charts depending on the CMs can be seen.

Figure_indicator Figure_chart

docs.title: Selection of tiles and working region
Selection of tiles
docs.text:
You can choose the territorial scale that you want to analyze (NUTS regions or hectare level) and set the tile of the map

There are the following configurations possible

  1. Zoom in and Zoom out the map
  2. Show the NUTS boundaries to select specific NUTS regions or use hectare to customize your selection
  3. Use OpenStreetMap tile or Satelite tile Go To Top

docs.title: Selection of working region
docs.text: Once the working tiles are selected, the map is displayed in showing spatial division in terms of the selected tiles. The user can select the region of interest.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
CoolLIFE is an open-source online tool designed to support energy planning at both local and national levels. It provides a quick estimate of cooling demand for any European region and assesses the local renewable energy potential to meet this demand. By using detailed data and CoolLIFE’s advanced calculation modules, you can develop more in-depth cooling strategies. This tool helps you make informed decisions for areas like villages, towns, cities, regions, or even individual buildings.

Developed by top European research institutions in collaboration with city administrations, CoolLIFE is your key to planning a climate-neutral energy future. Visit the website by clicking to explore your city’s energy potential.

Go To Top

docs.title: Accessing the toolbox
docs.text:
Once the toolbox is opened, the disclaiming page is shown. Besides the disclaimer message, additional information regarding supported browsers, the objectives of the CoolLIFE project, the link to the CoolLIFE project website and the data repositories is provided.

Fig. 1 Short introduction to CoolLIFE toolbox

The CoolLIFE GUI is a GIS-based interface. By closing the disclaiming page, the user sees the map of Europe. By default, the cold demand density map of EU-27 countries and NUTS 2 boundaries are depicted. In addition to these two maps, some tools and buttons can be seen in GUI. These tools are illustrated in the following figure.

Fig. 2 Initial Page

Go To Top

Here you can spot at first glance 3 parts of the user interface:

  1. a toolbar to the top left, the map itself,
  2. Layers ribon
  3. Tools ribon
  4. Calculation module ribon
  5. some tools for changing the style of the map.

docs.title: Upper Toolbar
docs.text:
• Link to the CoolLIFE website
  • Go To Place: zoom in to a specific region by typing the name,
  • Feedback: give us feedback regarding the tool
  • Connect: register and login into the web application in order to save your work

docs.title: Connect
docs.text: Here you can: - Create an account - Log-in into your account to save your progress - Or reset your password if you forget it

docs.title: Login
docs.text:
After registering and activating your account you should be able to login with your email and password (see Fig.4 below).

Fig.4.: Login Form

docs.title: Register
docs.text:
Here you can create an account for the CoolLIFE website. After submitting the form you will receive an email for activating your account. With your account, you will be able to save your progress.

Fig.5.: Registration Form

docs.title: Recover
docs.text:
If you ever forget your password you can recover it under this menu (see Fig.6. below). Please be aware of setting a new password afterward.

Fig.6.: Recover Form

docs.title: Go To Place
docs.text: You can zoom to a specified region by typing its name (see animation below)

docs.title: Tools Ribon
docs.text:
By Pressing this Ribon the selection tools will appear at the left of your screen.

The figure below shows the appearance of the tools:

  1. Tool for the selection of regions
  2. Create a custom square region
  3. Create a custom circle region
  4. Create a custom polygon region
  5. Upload a GeoJSON object to use a custom area selection

A. shows how many regions you selected B. shows the overall surface of the smallest rectangule that can cover the selected area C. shows the scale you selected from right toolbar

a. button for loading the results of your selected region and selected layers b. button for deleting a selected region

docs.title: Show Results
docs.text: Go To Top Depending on the layers and region you selected results for your configuration are shown in a sidebar at the right of your screen

docs.title: Feedback
docs.text: Do you have any suggestions on how to improve the tool? Have you noticed any errors? Please let us know! By giving us feedback you can help us improve the toolbox!

docs.title: Welcome Page (Introduction To The CoolLIFE Project)
CoolLIFE Toolbox
docs.text:
For decades, building space cooling (SC) demand has increased steadily in Europe (EU27) and is expected to rise even more in the coming years 2030/2050). These increasing cooling demand patterns highlight the need to prioritize cooling in the context of energy planning equally.

CoolLIFE offers an open-source online software that allows mapping of the SC demand of the EU27’s buildings from the hectare to continent level, providing indications to best meet actual and future demand while promoting the implementation of innovative, efficient SC solutions (best available technologies). The tool will also provide comfort, lifestyle, and user behaviour indications for different territories, demand-side management/demand response measures, as well as economic, policy, and legal/regulatory conditions.

With the application of the tool the user will be able to i.) map the SC demand of buildings (energy intensity levels – classified by specific energy demand in kWh/m2 y - and total energy need) at different spatial levels for the whole EU-27 (100 x 100 m, local administrative unit - LAU2, LAU1, the nomenclature of territorial units for statistics - NUTS3, 2, 1, and 0) as well as provide the user with knowledge on: ii.) how to cover the detected demand by local renewable energy sources (RES) and which technologies and measures could be utilized to cover the identified SC need – notably by innovative, integrated, holistic solutions/best available technologies (BATs) for SC purposes, iii.) data and information on how comfort, lifestyle, and user behaviour specificities in different geographical areas influence SC demand (for example energy need and air-conditioning patterns, culturally-determined comfort expectations, regional differences in work schedule structures, adaptive routine practices, among others), iv.) how feasible from an economic point of view the selected technologies/measures are (through CBA indications), as well as v.) legal and regulatory measures to be taken into consideration concerning selected interventions. Moreover, the CoolLIFE tool will inform the user also about the possibility to apply vi.) demand response (DR), shifting load from peak to off-peak hours or maximizing photovoltaics (PV)-self-consumption by SC devices.

The CoolLIFE Toolbox was developed by leading research institutions across Europe together with city administrations. Go on the website by clicking [HERE]<<>> and discover your city’s climate-neutral energy future. Go To Top

docs.title: CoolLIFE WIKI
docs.text:
The CoolLIFE Wiki hosts the documentation, guidance and manual of the CoolLIFE toolbox and Knowledge HUb. It consists of the following main parts:

  1. Data sets,
  2. General toolbox functionalities,
  3. Calculation modules,
  4. How to apply the CoolLIFE Tool (Guidelines)
  5. Calculation Modules,
  6. Learning Centre. These sections are accessible in all Wiki pages in the sidebar.

The Data sets section provides information about CoolLIFE data set repositories as well as methodologies for gathering these data sets.

The General tool functionalities and structure section guides the user through the interface of the toolbox. The section covers all general aspects of the toolbox, which are related to the user experience, e.g. navigating through different parts of the toolbox, layer selection, retrieving indicators, data upload and export functionalities etc.

The 'How to apply CoolLIFE Tool' provides use Cases for different target groups and guideline to perform cooling analyses and planning (Comprehensive Assessments, NECPs, local strategies)

The Calculation Modules section provides an in-depth explanation of concepts and methodologies behind the calculation modules. Besides the explanation of the methodology, the provided examples and test runs for each calculation module help the user to obtain an understanding of input parameters and output results.

Go To Top
